channel.title: NYT > Arts > Music
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: ‘Tristan und Isolde’ at the Met Opera Has a Troubling Problem
item.description: Audiences are packing the theater for a new “Tristan und Isolde.” Everyone can see the same spectacle, but they probably don’t hear the same sound.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone
item.media:description: Lise Davidsen as Isolde on Es Devlin’s set for “Tristan und Isolde.” From the Balcony, the performers, enveloped and hooded by the tunnels, sound dampened.

item.title: D. Boon’s Death Shook Punk Rock. 40 Years Later, Minutemen Look Back.
item.description: The influential band’s singer and guitarist died at 27, but the bassist Mike Watt and the drummer George Hurley never lost his spirit.
item.dc:creator: Bob Mehr

item.title: Mozart Wouldn’t Be Mozart Without These Three Objects
item.description: Hear the instruments and scores, on view in a new exhibition at the Morgan Library and Museum, that proved foundational for Mozart’s life in music.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone and Karsten Moran
item.media:credit: Karsten Moran for The New York Times

item.title: 11 Robyn Bangers for the Pop Star’s Long-Awaited Return
item.description: Prepare for her first album since 2018, “Sexistential,” with 49 minutes of up-tempo gems.
item.dc:creator: Caryn Ganz
item.media:credit: Ana Cuba for The New York Times
item.media:description: Robyn is back with “Sexistential,” her first new album since 2018.

item.title: Salzburg Festival Fires Its Artistic Director
item.description: The board terminated Markus Hinterhäuser’s contract early, leaving the leadership of the world’s largest classical music festival in limbo for now.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone and Alex Marshall
item.media:description: Markus Hinterhäuser, who has been effectively dismissed as artistic director of the Salzburg Festival in Austria, in a 2021 photo.

item.title: FKA twigs Says in Suit That Shia LaBeouf Had Her Sign ‘Illegal’ NDA
item.description: The suit said the nondisclosure agreement that was part of her court settlement with Mr. LaBeouf, her former boyfriend, effectively silenced her. His lawyer disputes that.
item.dc:creator: Melena Ryzik
item.media:credit: Andy Kropa (FKA Twigs) and Lewis Joy/Invision, via Associated Press
item.media:description: The filing is the latest chapter in a long legal battle between the singer FKA twigs, left, and her former boyfriend, the actor Shia LaBeouf.

item.title: Dash Crofts, of the Soft-Rock Duo That Sang ‘Summer Breeze,’ Dies at 87
item.description: Seals & Crofts had a number of Billboard Top 20 songs in the 1970s before their chart topper brought them global fame.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:description: Dash Crofts performing with Seals & Crofts in Atlanta in 1977. The duo had a string of hits in the ’70s, including “Diamond Girl,” “Get Closer” and “We May Never Pass This Way Again.”

item.title: Chip Taylor, Writer of ‘Wild Thing’ and Other Rock Hits, Dies at 86
item.description: The brother of the actor Jon Voight, he wrote songs for Frank Sinatra, Janis Joplin, Anne Murray and, with “Angel of the Morning,” Juice Newton.
item.dc:creator: Hannah Ziegler
item.media:description: Chip Taylor in 2003. “I wasn’t sure about ‘Wild Thing’ at first,” Mr. Taylor recalled in an interview in 1999. “On the demo I told the sound engineer to turn off the lights in the studio, and I just sat on a stool and sang it like a blues guy would.”

item.title: Jury Awards Chance the Rapper $35 in Legal Fight With Former Manager
item.description: The jury ruled in favor of the rapper, whose former manager, Pat Corcoran, was seeking $3.8 million in unpaid royalties.
item.dc:creator: Sopan Deb
item.media:credit: Rebecca Smeyne for The New York Times
item.media:description: Chance the Rapper in Brooklyn last year. A legal dispute with his former manager, Pat Corcoran, ended with the rapper being awarded $35 in damages. The pair never put their deal in writing.

item.title: Supreme Court Sides With Internet Provider in Copyright Fight Over Pirated Music
item.description: Leading music labels sued Cox Communications for failing to terminate accounts of subscribers flagged for distributing copyrighted music.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:description: At issue for the justices was whether providers like Cox could be held legally responsible if they knew that customers were pirating music but did not take steps to terminate their internet access.

item.title: Mike Vernon, Who Helped Spark the British Blues Boom, Dies at 81
item.description: He produced albums — by John Mayall & the Bluesbreakers, with Eric Clapton, and the early Fleetwood Mac — that defined 1960s blues rock. He also shepherded David Bowie’s debut album.
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams
item.media:description: Mike Vernon in 1979. He produced albums for Britain’s powerhouse Decca label as well as for his own label, Blue Horizon.

item.title: Robert White, Tenor Renowned for Irish Ballads, Dies at 89
item.description: A performer from childhood, he became a versatile singer in the mold of the Irish superstar John McCormack, adept at both classical repertoire and traditional songs.
item.dc:creator: James Barron

item.title: Is It Possible to Solve the Ending of Puccini’s ‘Turandot’?
item.description: This Orientalist classic, left unfinished, has had to be completed since Puccini’s death. A new version hands that task to artists with Asian roots.
item.dc:creator: Kevin Ng
item.media:credit: Hiroyuki Ito for The New York Times
item.media:description: Puccini’s “Turandot” at the Metropolitan Opera, in Franco Zeffirelli’s production.

item.title: ‘Who Doesn’t Love Elvis?’ Trump Takes an Unexpected Detour to Graceland.
item.description: President Trump mused about whether he could beat Elvis Presley in a fistfight and used a golden Sharpie to sign a replica of one of the singer’s guitars.
item.dc:creator: Chris Cameron
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump holding up a replica of an Elvis Presley guitar after signing it while visiting Graceland, the singer’s home in Memphis, on Monday.

item.title: Augie Meyers, Pioneer of Tex-Mex Music, Dies at 85
item.description: His carnival-like swirls on the Vox organ helped define the sound of the border with groups like the Sir Douglas Quintet and the Texas Tornados.
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams
item.media:description: Augie Meyers in 1983. His bright, syncopated riffs on the Vox organ functioned as the sonic engine of the Sir Douglas Quintet and set the model for countless groups to follow.

item.title: Review: On ‘Arirang,’ BTS Is Too Big to Fail. But Not Too Big to Succeed.
item.description: The K-pop superstars’ new album, “Arirang,” comes with huge commercial expectations. The group made room for artistic experimentation, too.
item.dc:creator: Jon Caramanica
item.media:description: The seven members of BTS returned to the stage on Saturday in Seoul (though one skipped most of the choreography: An injured RM was mostly confined to a stool).

item.title: K-Pop’s BTS Returns to the Stage
item.description: The group’s first concert in nearly four years — held in the historic center of Seoul — was heavy on songs from its new album. They also played a few old hits.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Jun Michael Park for The New York Times

item.title: The director of the Netflix livestream is a veteran of the Oscars and the Super Bowl.
item.description: Hamish Hamilton, a multiple-time Emmy and BAFTA winner, is directing the Netflix broadcast of BTS’s comeback concert on Saturday.
item.dc:creator: Jin Yu Young
item.media:description: Hamish Hamilton, an accomplished television director who specializes in live broadcasts, accepting an Emmy Award in 2024.

item.title: Behind the scenes of the BTS livestream.
item.dc:creator: Jin Yu Young
item.media:credit: Jun Michael Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gwanghwamun Square in Seoul, where the concert is taking place on Saturday.

item.title: Why BTS Is Promoting Its New Album, ‘Arirang,’ in Korea Before Hitting the Road
item.description: The last time BTS released an album, it kicked off with appearances in New York before Seoul. This time it’s the other way around.
item.dc:creator: Jeff Benjamin
item.media:credit: Jun Michael Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: BTS merchandise at a convenient store in Seoul on Saturday.

item.title: What BTS Wore for Its Comeback Concert and Why
item.description: The band’s looks, by the Korean brand Songzio, represented a bigger story about Korean culture and meaning.
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Friedman

item.title: Here’s how America met BTS.
item.description: The South Korean band broke into American pop culture by hitting the talk shows, performing on “Saturday Night Live” and appearing at the White House.
item.dc:creator: Francesca Regalado
item.media:description: Members of BTS joined the White House press secretary, Karine Jean-Pierre, at the White House, in 2022.

item.title: There was extra security as BTS fans arrived at the concert site.
item.dc:creator: Max Kim

item.title: One reason BTS went on hiatus? To do military service.
item.dc:creator: Choe Sang-Hun
item.media:description: BTS members RM, left, and V in Chuncheon, South Korea, last year, after their release from 18 months of military service.

item.title: These BTS Fans Waited 15 Hours to See Their Idols
item.description: Those without tickets arrived at the venue before the sun rose on Saturday to stake out areas that offered unobstructed views of the stage.
item.dc:creator: Yan Zhuang
item.media:description: International BTS fans waiting for the concert on Saturday, from left: Thycianne Santos, Corrine van der Moolen, Leimomi Kamiya, Betty Pietraszora, Kayla Sanchez, Niki Anchala and Emi Noguchi.

item.title: ​Gwanghwamun Square, the venue of the show, has long attracted crowds.
item.dc:creator: Choe Sang-Hun
item.media:credit: Chang W. Lee/The New York Times
item.media:description: A protest against then President Yoon Suk Yeol of South Korea in Gwanghwamun Square in 2024.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Jin Yu Young, Yan Zhuang, Jeff Benjamin and Max Kim

item.title: St. Vincent, Death Cab for Cutie: 8 Songs We’re Talking About This Week
item.description: St. Vincent teams with a 60-piece orchestra, and Squarepusher replaces one by playing all the parts himself.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles
item.media:credit: OK McCausland for The New York Times
item.media:description: St. Vincent’s “Live in London!” captures a performance conducted by Jules Buckley.

item.title: Bernard Rands, Pulitzer-Winning Composer, Dies at 92
item.description: His early successes in the 1960s, writing in the complex vein of high modernism, yielded later in his career to a more accessibly lyrical style.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter
item.media:credit: Evening Standard/Hulton Archive, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Mr. Rands in 1971. He evolved into a composer whose music was compared by critics to that of Debussy, with shimmering orchestral textures and hints of tonality.

item.title: Lights, Drones, Purple Pizza: BTS-mania Takes Over Seoul
item.description: The South Korean capital was aglow for the return of the K-pop supergroup.
item.dc:creator: Yan Zhuang
item.media:description: Drones form the image of Jin, a BTS member, in a light show over Seoul on Friday.

item.title: Gustavo Dudamel’s Portrait Emerges From a Season of Introductions
item.description: The New York Philharmonic’s next music leader, slowly revealing facets of his personality, used his latest visit to showcase his political conscience.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone
item.category: The People United Will Never Be Defeated! (Musical Work)

item.title: BTS Leans Into Korean History With ‘Arirang.’ Here Are the Major References.
item.description: The group’s new album “Arirang” includes a nod to a beloved Korean folk song and other important historical references.
item.dc:creator: Jin Yu Young
item.media:description: Trucks promoting the BTS comeback concert passing by the statue of King Sejong at Gwanghwamun Square in Seoul, on Thursday. The group performs at the square on Saturday.

item.title: What To Know About the BTS Comeback Concert Streaming Live on Netflix
item.description: The K-pop supergroup’s first show in over three years will stream live on Netflix at 7 a.m. Eastern time on Saturday. The New York Times will cover it live from Seoul.
item.dc:creator: Yan Zhuang
item.media:credit: Jun Michael Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gwanghwamun Square in Seoul on Saturday, hours before the BTS comeback concert.

item.title: Lukashenko Jailed Her in Belarus, but She Wants the World to Talk to Him
item.description: Maria Kalesnikava is campaigning for the West to engage with the regime in Belarus that imprisoned her for more than five years.
item.dc:creator: Valerie Hopkins
item.media:credit: Sergey Ponomarev for The New York Times
item.media:description: Maria Kalesnikava with her flute this week in Berlin, where she now lives in exile.

item.title: BTS Is Back With ‘Arirang,’ but the K-Pop Landscape Has Changed
item.description: The superstar boy band returns after a four-year hiatus on Friday. The genre it helped turn into a global juggernaut has endured some shifts, and minted new stars.
item.dc:creator: Steve Knopper
item.media:description: BTS onstage at the Grammys in 2022. The group is returning with its first album since that year on Friday.

item.title: Why K-Pop Is Hitting a Roadblock in China
item.description: Japanese members of some K-pop bands have been absent from recent concerts in China. Experts say that’s linked to tension between Tokyo and Beijing.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon
item.media:description: Shotaro, third left, at a Riize concert in Austin, Texas, last year.
